questions.passage:
For several centuries, European governments busied themselves with the pressing question of who might wear what. Sumptuary laws, as historians label them, fixed in exhausting detail the fabrics, colors, furs, and ornaments permitted to each rank of society. A merchant's wife might be forbidden the silk allowed to a knight's; a servant might face a fine for a slashed doublet above his station; certain dyes, certain furs, certain lengths of train were reserved by statute for those the law deemed entitled to them. Legislators returned to the subject again and again, revising, extending, and re-enacting their codes with a diligence that suggests, more than anything, how poorly the codes worked.

The stated justifications were various and mostly unconvincing. Some laws claimed to curb extravagance and protect households from ruining themselves on finery. Others invoked the balance of trade, fretting that coin was flowing abroad to buy foreign cloth. But the recurring and unmistakable motive was the preservation of visible hierarchy. In a world without identity documents, dress was a language, and rank was meant to be legible at a glance. The lord looked like a lord and the laborer like a laborer, and the whole apparatus of deference depended on one's being able to tell them apart across a crowded square.

What alarmed the legislators was that this legibility was breaking down. Commerce was minting wealth that did not correspond to birth, and the newly prosperous merchant, the successful artisan, the ambitious farmer's son, all wished to dress the part their purses could now afford. The sumptuary code was an attempt to freeze the sartorial order against the thaw of the market, to insist by statute that a man's coat should announce the estate he was born to rather than the fortune he had made. It was, in effect, an effort to keep money from talking.

It failed, and the manner of its failure is instructive. The laws were flouted almost as fast as they were passed. The wealthy commoner paid the fine and wore the silk anyway, treating the penalty as a license fee; enforcement was sporadic, arbitrary, and easily evaded; and fashion, which lives by novelty, simply moved faster than any legislature could follow, inventing new extravagances the old statutes had never thought to forbid. Each round of re-enactment testifies to the failure of the last. A law that must be passed a dozen times over is a law that no one is obeying.

Yet it would be a mistake to read the sumptuary codes merely as a record of futility. What they document, precisely by failing, is a society whose old certainties about who was who were dissolving under economic pressure the law could not withstand. Every statute forbidding a merchant the colors of a lord is evidence that merchants were wearing them, and that the boundary between the estates had grown porous enough to require policing. The codes are, in this sense, a photograph of anxiety, a running catalogue of exactly those transgressions the authorities most feared, preserved in the negative of what they tried to ban.

When such laws faded, as they did across Europe over the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, they faded less from repeal than from irrelevance. A commercial society had arrived in which wealth, not birth, increasingly set the terms, and the notion that the state should legislate the drape of a sleeve came to seem not merely unworkable but absurd. The sumptuary law died not because it was struck down but because the society it existed to preserve had already vanished. It survives now chiefly as a curiosity, and as a reminder that the most revealing laws are sometimes the ones that could not be enforced.
questions.stem: The passage argues that sumptuary laws are historically valuable chiefly because they

choices.A: reduced household extravagance and corrected imbalances in foreign trade
choices.B: successfully preserved the visible distinctions between the various social ranks for several uninterrupted centuries
choices.C: document, through their very failure, the anxieties of a hierarchy dissolving under commercial pressure
choices.D: were enforced consistently enough to shape how ordinary people dressed

explanations.A: The extravagance and trade justifications are described as mostly unconvincing pretexts; the author does not credit the laws with achieving those aims.
explanations.B: The passage insists the laws failed, flouted almost as fast as passed, so it cannot claim they successfully preserved rank distinctions.
explanations.C: Correct. The author calls the codes a photograph of anxiety, arguing that every statute forbidding a merchant a lord's colors is evidence that merchants wore them and that the boundary between estates had grown porous. Their value lies in what their failure records.
explanations.D: The author stresses sporadic, arbitrary, easily evaded enforcement, the opposite of a consistency that shaped how people dressed.

questions.stem: The author identifies the recurring real motive behind sumptuary laws as

choices.A: the desire to keep social rank legible at a glance in a world without identity documents
choices.B: a sincere and paternalistic concern to protect poorer households from ruining themselves on costly imported finery
choices.C: the wish to encourage domestic textile production over imports
choices.D: the aim of promoting novelty and variety in fashion

explanations.A: Correct. The passage names the recurring and unmistakable motive as the preservation of visible hierarchy, since dress was a language and rank was meant to be legible at a glance in a world without identity documents.
explanations.B: Protecting households from ruin is listed as a stated justification the author calls mostly unconvincing, not the recurring real motive.
explanations.C: The balance-of-trade worry is another of the stated and doubted pretexts, not the underlying motive the author identifies.
explanations.D: Fashion's novelty is cited as a reason the laws failed, not as a goal the legislators pursued; they sought to restrain display, not promote it.

questions.stem: The remark that a wealthy commoner treated the penalty 'as a license fee' illustrates

choices.A: that fines were set high enough to deter all violations
choices.B: how easily the affluent absorbed fines and evaded the codes' intent
choices.C: that only the poor were ever punished under the statutes
choices.D: that the laws were enforced with unusual severity

explanations.A: Treating a fine as a license fee shows the penalty did not deter; the passage offers it as evidence of failure, not of effective deterrence.
explanations.B: Correct. The image appears among the reasons the laws failed: the wealthy commoner paid the fine and wore the silk anyway, treating the penalty as a routine cost rather than a deterrent, one instance of the codes being flouted and evaded.
explanations.C: The example concerns a prosperous commoner shrugging off the fine, so it does not support the claim that only the poor were punished.
explanations.D: The surrounding text describes sporadic and arbitrary enforcement, contradicting any picture of unusual severity.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the legislators who repeatedly re-enacted sumptuary codes is best described as

choices.A: neutral, merely recording the statutes without any evaluation
choices.B: admiring of their determination to uphold social order
choices.C: outraged at the cruelty of the penalties they imposed
choices.D: wry, treating their persistence as unwitting proof of their failure

explanations.A: The author plainly evaluates, calling the justifications unconvincing and the enterprise absurd by its end, so the stance is not neutral recording.
explanations.B: The author does not admire the effort; it is portrayed as futile, an attempt to keep money from talking that the market defeated.
explanations.C: The passage expresses no outrage over cruelty; its interest is in the laws' ineffectiveness and what that reveals, not in the harshness of penalties.
explanations.D: Correct. The author observes that a law which must be passed a dozen times over is a law no one is obeying, treating the legislators' diligence as evidence of how poorly the codes worked. The irony is deliberate and sustained.

questions.stem: According to the passage, sumptuary laws ultimately faded because

choices.A: the commercial world they were meant to hold back had already displaced the old order
choices.B: the courts at last struck them down as unacceptable violations of ordinary individual liberty and free choice
choices.C: enforcement finally became strict enough to make them unnecessary
choices.D: fashion ceased to change and the codes no longer needed revision

explanations.A: Correct. The author writes that the laws faded less from repeal than from irrelevance, once a commercial society had arrived in which wealth rather than birth set the terms, so the world the codes were meant to preserve had already gone.
explanations.B: The passage explicitly says they died not because they were struck down but because their world had vanished, ruling out formal judicial repeal as the cause.
explanations.C: Enforcement is described as chronically weak; the laws faded from irrelevance, not from a sudden success in enforcement.
explanations.D: Fashion's relentless change is cited as a reason the laws failed, not as something that stopped; the codes faded because the social order shifted.

questions.stem: The claim that the codes form 'a photograph of anxiety' functions in the argument to

choices.A: suggest that the statutes were written mainly for their artistic qualities
choices.B: concede that the laws achieved their intended preservation of hierarchy
choices.C: reinterpret the laws' failure as valuable evidence of the transgressions authorities feared
choices.D: prove that the authorities were largely indifferent to the shifting relationship between new wealth and inherited rank

explanations.A: Nothing in the passage attributes artistic intent to the statutes; the photograph image is about what the bans inadvertently record, not aesthetics.
explanations.B: The metaphor accompanies the argument that the laws failed; it does not concede they preserved hierarchy but explains what their failure reveals.
explanations.C: Correct. The phrase turns apparent futility into documentary worth: each ban records exactly the transgressions authorities most feared, preserved in the negative of what they tried to forbid. It is the hinge by which failure becomes evidence.
explanations.D: The image shows authorities acutely anxious about shifting wealth and rank, the opposite of indifference.
